entry.title: BIP 118 and SIGHASH_ANYPREVOUT
entry.summary:
The discussion centers around a theoretical implementation of the Decker-Russell-Osuntokun (DRO) construction in cryptocurrency protocols, specifically using a single internal taproot public key, denoted as `P = MuSig(A, B)`. This setup would involve `A` and `B`, two parties in a channel, where the funding outpoint is spent with a taprooted `P` combined with a straightforward tapscript. This script checks for signatures, effectively allowing transaction updates to be signed by the internal taproot public key utilizing `SIGHASH_ANYPREVOUTANYSCRIPT`.
In this proposed model, every update transaction output becomes spendable through a taprooted `P` plus an incrementally indexed tapscript that includes operations for checking lock time and dropping it, followed by signature verification. The design ensures that each update transaction incorporates a `nLockTime` that increases monotonically, which ties back to its unique index value.
Moreover, state transactions in this framework are exclusively signed with the internal taproot public key under `SIGHASH_ANYPREVOUT`, thereby committing to a specific script that correlates directly to an individual update transaction's unique script sequence. This mechanism restricts a state transaction to only spend from its designated update transaction. Additionally, the input of a state transaction adheres to a `nSequence` that enforces a relative locktime reflecting the agreed unilateral close delay between parties.
This approach underscores a cryptographic method known as MuSig signing, necessitating 1.5 round trips for a two-party channel configuration or three broadcast rounds for constructions involving three or more participants. This information hints at both the complexity and the cryptographic rigor required to implement such a system securely within blockchain technologies.
